Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time on weekends to catch up since I missed a lot of classes, but it never felt consistent. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend hours making color-coded notes and diagrams from the lecture videos when I could access them. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could actually sit down and focus without distractions.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling emotionally. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, logging in multiple times, and other weeks I just couldn't bring myself to open anything. Deadlines definitely pushed me into action, but my anxiety would sometimes paralyze me before I even started. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but life just didn't allow for that consistency.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification on my phone that reminded me something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so low. The discussion posts also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed connecting with other students when I had the energy. Other times, I'd log in during a mindfulness break just to feel like I was doing something productive.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me, usually in the evenings after dinner. I think it happened because I started associating it with watching the video lectures, which I actually found helpful. But then things got overwhelming with my anxiety spiking, and that routine fell apart. It's hard to maintain habits when you're constantly fighting your own mind.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm such a visual learner and I missed so many actual classes. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to sketch out diagrams and concepts. The discussion forums were also surprisingly helpful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. Those peer interactions made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs because I just couldn't focus on walls of text. With my anxiety being so high, anything that felt like extra work got pushed aside. I also delayed a lot of the practice quizzes because I was scared of seeing a bad score and spiraling. It wasn't that they weren't useful—I just couldn't handle the emotional weight of potentially failing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it helped things click visually in my brain. If I watched a video and suddenly understood a concept I'd been struggling with, that felt like a win. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because there was human connection involved. Anything that felt like busy work or didn't have clear purpose just drained me more than it helped.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford emotionally. Required ones came with deadline pressure that forced me to act despite my anxiety, but optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them and never did. There was also this guilt with optional activities—like I should be doing them but couldn't, which made me feel worse. Required activities at least had external accountability that pushed through my paralysis.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access and then study from my phone or printed notes later. I made tons of handwritten diagrams and flashcards that I'd review on the bus or during breaks at my part-time job. A lot of my learning happened in spaces and times where the LMS couldn't track me at all.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was genuinely studying hard but barely touched the LMS. I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes, redrawing diagrams from memory, or explaining concepts out loud to myself. Sometimes I'd study with a friend from class using their notes since I'd missed lectures. The LMS would show me as basically inactive, but I was actually putting in real effort offline.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, download what I could, and then rewatch sections offline multiple times. With my limited resources and inconsistent internet, it made more sense to access things once and then work with them independently. My phone had a whole folder of screenshots from lecture slides that I'd flip through during downtime. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. When my anxiety peaked, even opening my laptop felt overwhelming. I remember one stretch where I was just so sad and exhausted that I couldn't make myself care about anything academic. Financial stress at home also pulled my attention away—it's hard to focus on coursework when you're worried about basic stuff.
Q13: Workload and fatigue hit me hard, especially combined with my high anxiety. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities like my extracurriculars and helping out at home meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of energy I had left. Confusion about course material would sometimes make me avoid the LMS entirely because I didn't know where to even start.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd have a lecture video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere, spiraling about something unrelated. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open an assignment, look at it for twenty minutes, and close it without typing a single word. The LMS would log that time as engagement, but I wasn't really there mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. My anxiety would build up around them until they felt insurmountable, and avoiding them felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I knew it was making things worse, but the immediate relief of not facing them was too tempting. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit, but they couldn't fully break that avoidance cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into a few panicked hours. The LMS probably shows these spikes really clearly. It wasn't healthy, but without that external pressure, my motivation was too low to push through the anxiety on my own.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But if feedback was critical or I got a lower score than expected, I'd spiral and avoid the course for days. I started checking grades with my heart pounding, which isn't a great way to learn. The mindfulness intervention helped me process feedback a bit better toward the end.
Q18: The course structure was honestly both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed with my low motivation. But the sheer number of online courses I was taking made everything blur together. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller chunks because tackling big assignments felt impossible with my anxiety levels.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited my visual learning style. Having discussion forums where I could interact with peers also helped me feel connected despite missing so many classes. What made it harder was when there were too many resources to sort through—I'd get overwhelmed and shut down. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that pattern makes total sense, actually. You can spend hours in the LMS watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand material, but still not feel confident enough to submit work. For me, the gap between understanding something and actually producing an assignment was huge because of my anxiety about being judged. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to translate that into finished products.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I performed well on exams because I studied hard offline in ways the LMS couldn't see. My exam score was strong even though my LMS activity and attendance were low. I think some students just learn differently and don't need to be constantly logged in to absorb material. The LMS captures one type of engagement but misses others completely.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on things, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the real reasons behind your behavior. A log might show I was inactive for a week but wouldn't show I was dealing with a mental health crisis.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm making connections between concepts and feeling curious rather than just anxious. It's also about feeling connected to the course community, even if that's just through online discussions. True engagement is mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still trying my best. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope. It also wouldn't show that despite everything—the low attendance, the incomplete assignments, the sadness—I genuinely cared about learning and worked really hard in my own way to earn that distinction.
